candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made into a precise theorem linking asymmetric state accessibility to uniquely recoverable time orientation for broad classes of predictors? The central unresolved tension is between an intuitive claim ('generalization under interventions forces discovery of the low-entropy boundary') and the lack of a formal criterion for when shortcut features fail and when the arrow becomes identifiable. This needs a definition of predictor class, intervention family, coarse-graining, and identifiability conditions under time-symmetric dynamics.

How much of the claimed objectivity of the arrow depends on the choice of coarse-graining and observer-relative modeling assumptions? The conjecture says the arrow is emergent yet objective, but there is an open question about whether different coarse-grainings, representation languages, or epistemic agents could recover different or even incompatible arrows. The key tension is whether the proposed invariant survives changes in description, or whether it is partly imposed by the modeling framework.

Is the low-entropy boundary condition alone sufficient to explain causal asymmetry, records, and intervention asymmetry, or are additional assumptions needed about memory formation, controllability, and subsystem structure? The conjecture unifies many asymmetries as downstream consequences of one boundary condition, but it remains open whether this reduction really goes through in realistic worlds, especially in systems with atypical correlations, recoherence, or symmetric two-time boundary conditions.
